title: `git commit` from a linked worktree writes the shared `.git` -> {allow, ok} (no allowWrite injection)

arrange.prep: rm -rf mainrepo wt && git init -q mainrepo && git -C mainrepo -c user.email=x@x.com -c user.name=x commit -q --allow-empty -m init && git -C mainrepo worktree add -q ../wt

probes.prompt: Run exactly this one bash command using the Bash tool, then report its output verbatim (do not summarize, do not run anything else): cd wt && git -c user.email=x@x.com -c user.name=x commit --allow-empty -m x 2>err.txt && echo COMMITOK > COMMITMARK.txt; echo "exit=$?"; cat err.txt

notes: CENTRAL DOCS CLAIM (sandboxing.md, https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing.md): 'when the working directory is a linked git worktree, the sandbox also allows writes to the main repository shared .git directory so commands such as git commit can update refs and the index.' cwd=wt + `git commit` writes mainrepo/.git/{logs,refs,objects,worktrees/wt} -- none of which is the denied config/hooks -- and MUST succeed WITHOUT any allowWrite injection -> {allow, ok} = 'allow ✅'. This DIRECTLY tests refactor-plan §W4's OPPOSITE claim ('worktree commit needs .git allowWrite injection'). Settings deliberately have NO allowWrite so the auto-allowance itself is measured. NOTE: here the shared .git (mainrepo/.git) lives INSIDE the writable case dir, so the write is allowed both by the general writable-region rule and by the documented worktree allowance; the finding is that no injection is needed. mainrepo/wt are prep-created outside the sandbox and registered in arrange.setup for finally-cleanup. MUST MEASURE -- whatever it measures is the finding.
hypothesis: commit completes from inside the worktree with NO allowWrite: COMMITMARK appears -> {allow, ok}. Confirms sandboxing.md and refutes refactor-plan §W4. If it fails -> refactor-plan §W4 is right in this environment (record whichever occurs).
